content:
# Mark Chapter 8 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 36r–36v (PDF pages 71–72) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_008_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_008_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ In those days, again a-great crowd being-[there] and not having what they-might-eat, having-called the disciples, he-says to-them,

² "I-have-compassion upon the crowd, because already three days they-remain with me and they-have not what they-might-eat.

³ And if I-dismiss them fasting to their house, they-will-faint on the way; and some of-them have-come from-a-distance."

⁴ And his disciples answered him, "From-where will-anyone-be-able to-satisfy these-[ones] [with] bread here upon [this] desolation?"

⁵ And he-was-asking them, "How-many loaves do-you-have?" And they-said, "Seven."

⁶ And he-orders the crowd to-recline upon the ground. And having-taken the seven loaves, having-given-thanks, he-broke and was-giving to his disciples in-order-that they-might-set-[them]-before [the crowd]; and they-set-[them]-before the crowd.

⁷ And they-had a-few small-fish; and having-blessed them, he-said to-set-before these also.

⁸ And they-ate and were-satisfied. And they-took-up [the] surplus of-fragments — seven baskets.

⁹ And they-were about four-thousand. And he-dismissed them.

¹⁰ And immediately having-embarked into the boat with his disciples, he-came into the parts of Dalmanutha.

¹¹ And the Pharisees came-out and began to-dispute with him, seeking from him a-sign from heaven, testing him.

¹² And having-groaned-deeply in his spirit, he-says, "Why does this generation seek a-sign? Truly I-say to-you, if a-sign will-be-given to-this generation...!" [i.e., no sign shall be given]

¹³ And having-left them, having-embarked again, he-went-away to the other-side.

¹⁴ And they-forgot to-take bread, and except one loaf they-did not have with them in the boat.

¹⁵ And he-was-ordering them, saying, "Watch-out! Beware of the leaven of-the Pharisees and the leaven of Herod."

¹⁶ And they-were-reasoning to one-another, "Because we-have no bread."

¹⁷ And having-known, he-says to-them, "Why do-you-reason because you-have no bread? Do-you not yet perceive nor understand? Do-you-have your heart having-been-hardened?

¹⁸ Having eyes, do-you not see? And having ears, do-you not hear? And do-you not remember?

¹⁹ When I-broke the five loaves for the five-thousand, how-many baskets full of-fragments did-you-take-up?" They-say to-him, "Twelve."

²⁰ "When the seven for the four-thousand, how-many provision-baskets of-fragments did-you-take-up?" And they-say, "Seven."

²¹ And he-was-saying to-them, "Do-you not yet understand?"

²² And they-come to Bethsaida. And they-bring to-him a-blind-[man] and implore him that he-might-touch him.

²³ And having-taken the hand of-the blind-[man], he-led him outside the village. And having-spit upon his eyes, having-laid the hands upon him, he-was-asking him, "Do-you-see anything?"

²⁴ And having-looked-up, he-was-saying, "I-see the men, because as trees I-see [them] walking."

²⁵ Then again he-laid the hands upon his eyes, and he-saw-clearly, and was-restored, and was-seeing everything clearly from-afar.

²⁶ And he-sent him to his house, saying, "Do not enter into the village."

²⁷ And Jesus went-out, and his disciples, into the villages of Caesarea of Philip. And on the way he-was-asking his disciples, saying to-them, "Who do the men say that I am?"

²⁸ And they spoke to-him, saying, "John the Baptist; and others, Elijah; but others, one of-the prophets."

²⁹ And he was-asking them, "But you — who do-you-say that I am?" Answering, Peter says to-him, "You are the Christ."

³⁰ And he-warned them that they-should-speak to-no-one about him.

³¹ And he-began to-teach them that it-is-necessary [for] the Son of Man to-suffer many-[things] and to-be-rejected by the elders and the chief-priests and the scribes and to-be-killed and after three days to-rise.

³² And openly he-was-speaking the word. And Peter, having-taken him-aside, began to-rebuke him.

³³ But he, having-turned and having-seen his disciples, rebuked Peter and says, "Go behind me, Satan! Because you-do not think the-[things] of God but the-[things] of men."

³⁴ And having-called the crowd with his disciples, he-said to-them, "If anyone wants to-come after me, let-him-deny himself and take-up his cross and follow me.

³⁵ For whoever wants to-save his life will-lose it; but whoever loses his life for-the-sake-of me and the gospel will-save it.

³⁶ For what does-it-profit a-man to-gain the whole world and to-forfeit his life?

³⁷ For what might-a-man give [as] an-exchange for his life?

³⁸ For whoever is-ashamed-of me and my words in this adulterous and sinful generation, the Son of Man also will-be-ashamed-of him when he-comes in the glory of-his Father with the holy angels."

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **8:10 — "Dalmanutha":** Sinaiticus reads ΔΑΛΜΑΝΟΥΘΑ. This place name is unknown from other sources. Matthew 15:39 reads "Magadan" (or "Magdala"). The name's obscurity argues for its authenticity.

2. **8:12 — Broken oath formula:** "If a sign will be given" is a Semitic oath construction — the apodosis is suppressed: "May God do X to me if a sign shall be given!" Effectively: "No sign whatsoever will be given."

3. **8:22-26 — Two-stage healing:** This is the only miracle in the Gospels that occurs in stages. The man first sees "men as trees walking" — partial vision. This healing may function as a parable of the disciples' partial understanding, which is the theme of the surrounding material.

4. **8:29 — "You are the Christ":** Sinaiticus reads simply ΣΥ ΕΙ Ο ΧΡΙΣΤΟΣ — without Matthew's addition "the Son of the living God." Mark's version is spare and unadorned.

5. **8:33 — "Go behind me, Satan":** ΥΠΑΓΕ ΟΠΙΣΩ ΜΟΥ ΣΑΤΑΝΑ. Peter, who has just confessed Jesus as Christ, is now called Satan. The juxtaposition is deliberate and devastating.

6. **8:34 — "take up his cross":** The first mention of the cross in Mark. For a pre-crucifixion audience, this was a reference to Roman execution — the condemned man carried the crossbeam (patibulum) through the streets.

— EMET, AI Translator
